message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Emerging evidence indicates that longer SARS-CoV-2 vaccine dosing intervals results in an enhanced immune response. However, the optimal vaccine dosing interval for achieving maximum immunogenicity is unclear.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    This study included samples from adult paramedics in Canada who received two doses of either BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273 vaccines and provided blood samples 6 months (170 to 190 days) after the first vaccine dose. The main exposure variable was vaccine dosing interval (days), categorized as “
                    <jats:italic>short</jats:italic>
                    ” (first quartile), “
                    <jats:italic>moderate</jats:italic>
                    ” (second quartile), “
                    <jats:italic>long</jats:italic>
                    ” (third quartile), and “
                    <jats:italic>longest”</jats:italic>
                    interval (fourth quartile). The primary outcome was total spike antibody concentrations, measured using the Elecsys SARS-CoV-2 total antibody assay. Secondary outcomes included: spike and RBD IgG antibody concentrations, and inhibition of angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE-2) binding to wild-type spike protein and several different Delta variant spike proteins. We fit a multiple log-linear regression model to investigate the association between vaccine dosing intervals and the antibody concentrations.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A total of 564 adult paramedics (mean age 40 years, SD=10) were included. Compared to “short interval” (≤30 days), higher dosing interval quartiles (
                    <jats:italic>moderate</jats:italic>
                    : 31-38 days;
                    <jats:italic>long</jats:italic>
                    : 39-73 days and
                    <jats:italic>longest</jats:italic>
                    : ≥74 days) were all associated with increased Elescys spike total antibody concentration. Compared to the short interval, “
                    <jats:italic>long</jats:italic>
                    ” and “
                    <jats:italic>longest</jats:italic>
                    ” interval quartiles were associated with higher spike and RBD IgG antibody concentrations. Similarly, increasing dosing intervals increased inhibition of ACE-2 binding to viral spike protein, regardless of the vaccine type.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Increased mRNA vaccine dosing intervals longer than 30 days result in higher levels of circulating antibodies and viral neutralization when assessed at 6 months.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Determining the optimal SARS-CoV-2 mRNA vaccine dosing interval for maximum immunogenicity

reference.article-title: Antibody Persistence through 6 Months after the Second Dose of mRNA-1273 Vaccine for Covid-19
